issue_title: The library should not cause a JSONDecodeError
issue_body:
Just got the following backtrace:

       r = client.lists.members.all(list_id=list_id, get_all=True, fields="members.id,members.status,members.last_changed,members.interests,members.merge_fields")
      File ".../mailchimp3/entities/listmembers.py", line 94, in all
        return self._iterate(url=self._build_path(list_id, 'members'), **queryparams)
      File ".../mailchimp3/baseapi.py", line 60, in _iterate
        result = self._mc_client._get(url=url, offset=0, count=5000, **queryparams)
      File ".../mailchimp3/mailchimpclient.py", line 29, in wrapper
        return fn(self, *args, **kwargs)
      File ".../mailchimp3/mailchimpclient.py", line 157, in _get
        raise MailChimpError(r.json())
      File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/requests/models.py", line 892, in json
        return complexjson.loads(self.text, **kwargs)
      File "/usr/lib/python3.6/json/__init__.py", line 354, in loads
        return _default_decoder.decode(s)
      File "/usr/lib/python3.6/json/decoder.py", line 339, in decode
        obj, end = self.raw_decode(s, idx=_w(s, 0).end())
      File "/usr/lib/python3.6/json/decoder.py", line 357, in raw_decode
        raise JSONDecodeError("Expecting value", s, err.value) from None
    json.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)

I can't be sure, but it appears to have been a transient problem with a MailChimp server.

Given that these things seem to happen, I think the library should do something reasonable, i.e. catch the JSON decode error and instead return the response. Something like

            if r.status_code >= 400:
                try:
                    error_data = r.json()
                except json.decoder.JSONDecodeError:
                    error_data = { "response": r }
                raise MailChimpError(error_data)

Then we get to see the actual error and can handle it, somehow, instead of getting back an opaque decode exception.

issue_title: 'ipv4addr' field needed for dhcpmember in creating network for old NIOS
issue_body:
In older NIOS like 6.11.7 (WAPI version 1.6), 'dhcpmember' requires 'ipv4addr' field.

Here is how this is implemented in older IPAM driver.

create_network in object manager:

        members_struct = []
        for member in members:
            members_struct.append({'ipv4addr': member.ip,
                                   '_struct': 'dhcpmember'})
        network_data['members'] = members_struct

"ipv4addr" field of "member" list is mandatory in 6.12.x build, whereas it is optional in 7.3 build.

We will need to support this in object manager.

issue_title: native_versioning broken with multiple statements in transaction
issue_body:
There are some behaviours in the generated trigger that are dubious:

    * If an `INSERT` is preceded by a statement within the transaction, the existing version is changed to an `Operation.UPDATE`. However the fields are set completely incorrectly - the `OLD.*` of an insert trigger would contain all-nulls. Currently for a field `name`, you get

          "name" = NEW."name", name_mod = name_mod
          OR OLD."name" IS DISTINCT FROM NEW."name"
    
      This would be better written as:

          "name" = NEW."name", name_mod = name_mod
          OR "name" IS DISTINCT FROM NEW."name"
      
      I.E. consider the `INSERT` which was preceded by a `DELETE` as an `UPDATE` instead and thus merge it properly.

    * `UPDATE` always forces the `operation_type` of an existing version to `Operation.UPDATE`. Since an `UPDATE` can only be preceded by an `INSERT` or an `UPDATE`, `operation_type` of an existing version should be kept intact.

   * `DELETE`, if preceded by changes in the transaction, will not change the `OperationType` - for a record that was updated and subsequently deleted, one could not find a version where it was deleted, at all!

   * If a `DELETE` stands alone, it sets the `_mod` attributes to `true` - unlike the case above. If one takes the `column_mod` to mean "this is a transaction that sets a new value" and the `column` as "this is the new value", it is wrong.

----

Thus I propose the following fixes:

     a) fix the upsert of `tg_op = 'INSERT`' to merge the updates properly.
     b) do not set the `operation_type` **at all** in the `UPDATE  of `tg_op = 'UPDATE'`
     c) the `DELETE` case: if a version already exists, and it is an `Operation.INSERT`, then **delete that version altogether**. Otherwise set its operation_type to `2`; no need to do anything else
     d) if a `DELETE` was the sole affecting statement, set the `_mod` fields to `false`.

This to me seems almost perfect, excepting the case that in an `UPDATE`, `DELETE` sequence, there will be some fields that have the `*_mod` set to `true` even though the record was then deleted.

issue_title: Native postgresql versioning is hardcoded to use `PropertyModTracker` but it is not documented.
issue_body: `sync_trigger` is [hardcoded](https://github.com/kvesteri/sqlalchemy-continuum/tree/1.2.1/sqlalchemy_continuum/dialects/postgresql.py#L492) (via [default arguments](https://github.com/kvesteri/sqlalchemy-continuum/blob/1.2.1/sqlalchemy_continuum/dialects/postgresql.py#L502)) to use `PropertyModTrackerPlugin`; this however is not [documented](http://sqlalchemy-continuum.readthedocs.org/en/latest/native_versioning.html).

issue_title: Replace tests_per_second with test_interval
issue_body:
Every time I'm looking on this option I do convertation from interval to frequency and vise versa. I think this is probably because intervals are widely used in programming. Also I have to pass fractional values when I want the check to happen once per two seconds or more.

For this specific task I think interval is more convenient because it is easy to compare interval with hanging time, while it's hard to compare frequency with time without doing convertation.

issue_title: Idea: expose more details on pagination
Add support for GitLab style diff shorthand and link shortening
issue_body:
GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket all have a way to diff commits.  GitLab has a shorthand that allows you to reference a diff: `{hash1}..{hash2}`, `repo@{hash1}..{hash2}`, or `user/repo@{hash1}..{hash2}`.

For link shortening, this is the format for the 3 providers:

- https://bitbucket.org/<user>/<repo>/branches/commits/<hash1>..<hash2>#diff (also can use `%0D` for some reason instead of `..`.
-https://github.com/<user>/<repo>/compare/<hash1>...<hash2>
-https://gitlab.example.com/<user>/<repo>/compare/<hash1>...<hash2>

I believe you can actually use any reference, but we will definitely stick to hashes only for shorthand, and may or may not allow other refs other than hash for link shortening.

issue_title: `Arithmatex`: Allow disabling of "smart dollars"
issue_body:
Create an operation mode which protects Math Blocks as in LaTeX.
Namely it should target advanced users who are aware of the edge cases.
It should work well when `MathJaX` is configured to handle only given elements and not scan the `body` for delimiters.

### Inline Math 
 - `\( ... \)` / `\(...\)`.
 - `$ ... $` / `$...$`.

### Display Math
 - `\[ ... \]` / `\[...\]`.
 - `$$ ... $$` / `$$...$$`.

Take care of cases there is line separating them. Something like:
```
$$
...
$$
```

### Environments
 - `\begin{envName} ... \end{envName}` / `\begin{envName}...\end{envName}`
 - Separated by line:
```
\begin{envName}
...
\end{envName}
```

There is also the cases environment is embedded inside *Display Math*:

```
$$\begin{envName}
...
\end{envName}$$
```

Or

```
$$ \begin{envName}
...
\end{envName} $$
```

### Dollar Sign
The Dollar Sign should be escaped: `\$` as in LaTeX.

If needed, I can create an `md` file as a test case.

issue_title: Keep duplicate of root-ref in its own directory (future proof).
issue_body:
1. Sometimes people edit URLs to go to the latest version (e.g. they're on v1.0.1 and they know v2.0.0 is the latest so they go there directly). If v2.0.0 is the latest they'll get a 404 since it's the root ref. Once v2.0.1 is out then /v2.0.0/index.html works.

2. When the developer switches root ref to master until the next release, /v2.0.0/index.html will exist. Then they switch back the root ref to greatest tag and now /v2.0.0/index.html doesn't work until they make the release.

Solution: when building, build the root ref twice. Once in the root and again in /<name>.

issue_title: coalesce() incorrectly expands constant values
issue_body:
When you pass string constant into coalesce, it will be expanded as identifier instead of string constant:
`db().select(db.table.str_field.coalesce('foo'))`
would expand into this:
`SELECT COALESCE(table.str_field,foo) FROM table`
but the expected behavior is this:
`SELECT COALESCE(table.str_field,'foo') FROM table`

issue_title: how to share parameters while my network is a bit fancy?
issue_body:
I am fresh here. I want to design a layer which contains two nets named net1 and net2. Net1 and Net2 share the same parameters. Here is my code.
```
from mxnet.gluon import nn
from mxnet import nd
from mxnet import gluon
import sys
sys.path.append('..')
import utils
import scipy.io as scio
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gpu
from mxnet import cpu
def vgg_block(num_convs, channels):
    out = nn.Sequential()
    for _ in range(num_convs):
        out.add(nn.Conv2D(channels=channels, kernel_size=(1,3), strides=(1,1), padding=(1,1), activation='relu'))
    out.add(nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=2, strides=2))
    return out
num_outputs = 10
architecture =  ((1,64), )
def vgg_stack(architecture):
    out = nn.Sequential()
    for (num_convs, channels) in architecture:
        out.add(vgg_block(num_convs, channels))
    return out


class Net(nn.Block):
    def __init__(self,**kwargs):
        super(Net, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        self.net = nn.Sequential()
        with self.net.name_scope():
            self.net.add(
                vgg_stack(architecture),
                nn.Flatten(),
                nn.Dense(10, activation="relu"),
                nn.Dropout(.5),
                nn.Dense(10, activation="relu"),
                nn.Dropout(.5),
                nn.Dense(10))

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.net(x)
        return x
class Mynn(nn.Block):
    def __init__(self,**kwargs):
        super(Mynn, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        self.net1 = nn.Sequential()
        with self.net1.name_scope():
            self.net1.add(Net())
        self.net2 = nn.Sequential()
        with self.net2.name_scope():
            self.net2.add(Net(params=self.net1.params))
        self.net3 = nn.Sequential()
        with self.net3.name_scope():
            self.net3.add(nn.Dense(100, activation="relu"),nn.Dropout(.5),nn.Dense(10))
    def forward(self, x1 , x2):
        x = self.net1(x1) - self.net2(x2)
        x = self.net3(x)
        return x
ctx = [cpu(0)]
mynn = Mynn()
mynn.initialize(ctx=ctx)
x1 = nd.random.uniform(shape=(10,9,1,1000))
x2 = nd.random.uniform(shape=(10,9,1,1000))
outputs = mynn(x1,x2)
```
Then , I print the parameters.
```
all_paras = mynn.collect_params()
print (all_paras['sequential0_sequential0_conv0_weight'].data())
print (all_paras['sequential1_sequential0_conv0_weight'].data()) 
```
It shows that, the net1 and net2 have different parameters.

issue_title: Why MXNet update parameters according to the key in dist_async？
issue_body: Traditional asynchronous, parameter server update the model parameter as a whole, but MXNet update parameter according to the key, in the case of multimachine, this way will lead to the case a worker pull down the model parameters but its keys are not the same version, why MXNet implement dist_async in this way? The model convergence can be better?

issue_title: Memory optimization in GLUON
issue_body:
Related user issue - https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/10382
* Supporting Memonger with GLUON will help in memory optimization. Memonger is currently supported only with Symbolic mode.
* There is a new environment variable - `MXNET_GPU_MEM_POOL_TYPE` introduced for Memory pool strategy which significantly reduces memory usage in - https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/11041 We need to test/experiment and document this feature.

issue_title: How do I extract layers and their "functionality" from pre-trained network?
issue_body:
## Description: 
I need to make a Siamese network for image similarity, and to do so I'm hoping to use 2 copies of Resnet152. Sorry, I made a separate post earlier about a slightly similar problem (about sharing weights). However, after reading a few more related issues, it seems like the best option is to make new layers and copy the weights over, set the weights to the same variable (for weight sharing) and then fine-tune it. 

So now my question is: How do I do that? I can get weights from arg_params but how do I know whether the layer is convolutional/fully connected/etc? Also, can the Load initializer load into a variable or does it have to load into a model? 

I also can't find how to get the names from Symbol and am pretty hopeless with reading documentation tbh, and couldn't figure out where this could be supported. 

Here's how I'm imagining it to work, but I'm sure it doesn't (error says the convolution layer doesn't have the right number of inputs or something):

```
sym, arg_params, aux_params = mx.model.load_checkpoint('resnet-152', 0)
layer = sym.get_internals()
# Load pretrained model
# How do I ensure that the weights are the same? Will this way work?
sym, arg_params, aux_params = mx.model.load_checkpoint('resnet-152', 0)
layer = sym.get_internals()
print type(sym)
print type(arg_params), len(arg_params)
print type(aux_params), len(aux_params)

# initialize
nn_layer_1 = [mx.sym.Variable('input_1')]
nn_layer_2 = [mx.sym.Variable('input_2')]
for name, args in arg_params.items():
    var = mx.sym.Variable(name+'_1')
    var = mx.initializer.Load(args.asnumpy()) # ??? how to initialize specific variable
    in1 = nn_layer_1[-1]
    in2 = nn_layer_2[-1]
    # initialize var to args
    nn_layer_1.append(mx.sym.Convolution(in1, weight=var, no_bias=True))
    nn_layer_2.append(mx.sym.Convolution(in2, weight=var, no_bias=True))

# fe_sym = mx.sym.Concat(layer1['flatten0_output'], layer2['flatten0_output_2'],dim=0)
fe_sym = mx.sym.Concat(nn_layer_1[-1], nn_layer_2[-1],dim=0)
```

Please help, and thanks in advance!

issue_title: Implementing New Operators
issue_body:
I would like to implement a new operator with MXNet, and the operator is already implemented with Caffe. What is the easiest way to port my Caffe implementation to MXNet?

The comment https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/7652#issuecomment-325894475 says that it is easier to implement complex operators using the legacy interface. But will the **legacy interface** be unsupported in the future? If it is OK to use the legacy interface, can someone provide links to some **comprehensive tutorials or examples**?

-------

To use the new nnvm interface for defining operators, I read this document: [A Beginner's Guide to Implementing Operators in MXNet Backend](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/docs/how_to/add_op_in_backend.md). The step-by-step explanations are clear and I have understood the general pipeline of defining a new operator, but there still seems to be some obstacles in the way.

1. How can I define **_variables storing intermediate results_** which can be used in both forward and backward computations? These variables will act like class member variables (assuming the operator is defined as a class). This is similar to the question in the comment https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/7652#issuecomment-338910634.
2. How can I define a _**operator with learnable parameters**_ (e.g. convolution operator)? The current implementations of operators such as convolution seem to use a different (and legacy) interface. And the [tensor examples](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/tree/master/src/operator/tensor) are all parameter-free operators like dot product.
3. Where can I find _**explanations of the various macros**_ to be used in operator definitions? The many macros are very confusing and it is hard for me to determine when I should use what and how. It would be very helpful if some templates for typical usages can be provided and explained.
4. How can I **_correctly reference the headers_** in [src/operator](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/tree/master/src/operator), if I put my operator definition files in an independent directory outside MXNet? Many source files simply use relative paths, e.g. `#include #include "../mxnet_op.h"` and `#include "../mshadow_op.h"`, which are not suitable if a file is located outside MXNet directories.
5. How can I **_specify multiple paths for the_** `EXTRA_OPERATORS` in  [config.mk](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/make/config.mk)? After checking the [Makefile](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/Makefile) and [CMakeLists.txt](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/CMakeLists.txt), I find that only one path is supported (Please correct me if I am wrong).

issue_title: Embedding(sparse_grad=True) + hybridization fallback issues
issue_body:
With the mxnet master branch below code will fail. The combination of the following causes the failure:

- Use of Embedding(sparse_grad=True) + other operations in one HybridBloc
- Combining multiple forward passes during a single autograd record session

```import mxnet as mx


class Embedding(mx.gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, num_tokens, embedding_size):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_tokens = num_tokens

        with self.name_scope():
            self.embedding = mx.gluon.nn.Embedding(
                num_tokens, embedding_size, sparse_grad=True)

    def hybrid_forward(self, F, words):
        emb = self.embedding(words)
        return emb + F.ones_like(emb)


ctx = mx.cpu()
embedding = Embedding(1000, 300)
embedding.initialize(ctx=ctx)
embedding.hybridize()

loss_function = mx.gluon.loss.SigmoidBinaryCrossEntropyLoss()
with mx.autograd.record():
    emb_in = embedding(mx.nd.arange(10, ctx=ctx))
    emb_in_2 = embedding(mx.nd.arange(10, ctx=ctx))
    loss = emb_in.sum() + emb_in_2.sum()
loss.backward()

print(embedding.embedding.weight.grad().data)
```

img = img.reshape(img.shape[0], 3, 7, 1)
dataLoader = g.data.DataLoader(img, batch_size=1)

net = g.nn.Sequential()
with net.name_scope():
#    net.add(g.nn.Conv1DTranspose(channels=3, kernel_size=3, activation='relu'))
    net.add(g.nn.Conv2DTranspose(channels=3, kernel_size=(3,1), activation='relu'))

net.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Normal(sigma=0.05))

for data in dataLoader:
    img = data.astype('float32')
    img = img.as_in_context(ctx)
    output = net(img)
==== 2D code end ====

Changed to 1D case, kernel dies every time:
==== 1D code ====
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon as g
import numpy as np

ctx = mx.cpu()

img = np.zeros((1, 3, 7))
# img = img.reshape(img.shape[0], 3, 7, 1)
dataLoader = g.data.DataLoader(img, batch_size=1)

net = g.nn.Sequential()
with net.name_scope():
    net.add(g.nn.Conv1DTranspose(channels=3, kernel_size=3, activation='relu'))
#    net.add(g.nn.Conv2DTranspose(channels=3, kernel_size=(3,1), activation='relu'))

net.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Normal(sigma=0.05))

for data in dataLoader:
    img = data.astype('float32')
    img = img.as_in_context(ctx)
    output = net(img)
==== 1D code end ====

Is there any trick for the Conv1DTranspose usage?

Thanks.

issue_title: Feed data into intermediate layer in forward pass
issue_body:
Is there a way to feed data into an intermediate symbol in the forward pass without constructing a new graph, similar to how you can use a feed_dict in tensorflow to feed data into every node of the computation graph?

I.e., conceptually, I would like to do something like this:
```
A=mx.sym.Variable('A')
B=mx.sym.Variable('B')
C=mx.sym.add_n(A, B, name='C')
D=mx.sym.Variable('D')
E=C*D
executor=E.bind(mx.cpu(), {'C':mx.nd.array([2]), 'D':mx.nd.array([3])})
```

My use case is a pretrained network, for which I would like to compute a forward pass for either a new input X or some feature representation at an intermediate layer (which might come from another network or some processed representation.

I presume there should be some way to do this; however, I have not been able to find it. Sorry if this question has already been answered elsewhere

issue_title: Can I pass non-tensor data as a c++ object through layers?
issue_body: I want to implement a layer which can output non tensor data as a c++ object that is stored in cpu memory. In caffe, I can store the object as a data member of a layer, and pass the address of the object in a Blob to subsequent layers. Storing the memory address in a Blob is a hack but it works, because in caffe every thread has separate layer instances, and the computation of a minibatch doesn't cross over different threads/machines. Can I do the same thing in mxnet?

issue_title: Retry on fetching file Content-Length
Item ids must be sequential starting at zero
issue_body:
It was non-obvious to me that the integer ids supplied to add_item() must be sequential and start at zero. But if they are not, get_n_items() returns bogus counts. Is this the intended contract?  It seems like if the ids are simply increasing sequential integers that AnnoyIndex would manage incrementing the ids.  Why have to specify it in add_item() at all? Am I missing something?

For example, this appears to work:
    >>> t = annoy.AnnoyIndex(3)
    >>> t.add_item(0, [1, 2, 3])
    >>> t.add_item(1, [2, 2, 3])
    >>> t.add_item(2, [3, 2, 3])
    >>> t.build(1)
    True
    >>> print(t.get_n_items())
    3

But use arbitrary ids, and the counts are tricked:
    >>> t = annoy.AnnoyIndex(3)
    >>> t.add_item(1, [1, 2, 3])
    >>> t.build(1)
    True
    >>> print(t.get_n_items())
    2
Or:
    >>>
    >>> t = annoy.AnnoyIndex(3)
    >>> t.add_item(100, [1, 2, 3])
    >>> t.build(1)
    True
    >>> print(t.get_n_items())
    101

issue_title: Possibility to mask columns
issue_body:
Hi, this is more of a feature request than an issue. Would it be possible to specify a mask when searching, so that only specified columns would be taken into account when finding nearest neighbours?
Let's say I have an index of buildings in a city, with each building being a vector with several values. Some may be transportation-related, some security-related, etc. I'd like to be able to find nearest neighbours for a group of values, without generating a separate index for them, which is of course always an option, but memory-intensive for a big city.
Thanks!

issue_title: FBX import scene hierarchy is broken by unnamed nodes
issue_body:
I briefly tested the commit 6a0dc071a967be9d69ed86228f21be04dfec23d6 cloned on April 9th 2018, and had many problems with FBX import and hierarchies. These problems are not present in the latest release 4.1.0.

I was importing mostly FBX files, and it seems that with deeper hierarchies Assimp would insert unnamed parent nodes containing the transform of their child, and then their child node would have the node name (as written in 3DS Max or Maya).

This was especially visible with animations where each bone would have an unnamed parent (containing the transform). But it also happens on files which don't have animations.

So for most deep nodes we get this:
> The usual $AssimpFbx$_PreRotation etc. nodes
> Some parent which has a proper name and transform
>----unnamed parent node with transform
>-------- its child node, which has the node name, but identity transform

I'm not sure if this is some new feature, or just broken stuff in master, but it would be nice to know if this can be fixed, or if not, the reasons for the change.

If this is a bug, I would expect the hierarchy to roughly match the hierarchy that was set in 3DSMax or Maya, and not contain those unnamed parent nodes, like it does in Assimp 4.1.0.

issue_title: OBJ loader returns non-white default color
issue_body:
I am loading an OBJ form a location that does not have mtl file, but has PNG texture with same name as the OBJ, like `model.obj`, `model.png`. Assimp actually picks up this texture as a material which is great!

The problem is that at the same time the material's diffuse color is set to `(0.6, 0.6, 0.6, 1.0)` and this is a problem since in our setup we multiply color of model with color of texture and it makes all the models darker.

In this case the `ai_mat->Get(AI_MATKEY_COLOR_DIFFUSE, color)` should return `AI_FAILURE`, instead of arbitrary default color. Or, the default color should be settable and white by default. Now, it is not possible to distinguish between no diffuse color and color equal to (0.6, 0.6, 0.6, 1.0).

Other color types such as ambient and missive are also defined and set to (0, 0, 0, 1).

I have found some Collada loader code that has these defaults: https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/f0bed87e0787c9f3bcd654ae84e990889f7e59c9/code/ColladaHelper.h#L559

The trick is to extract the PoEs from the annual frequency of exceedence,
```
poe = 1 - exp(-afe * t)

where `t` is the risk investigation time.

issue_title: Add a clear error in classical_damage if there is a PoE = 1

This happens for very low levels (or for NoDamageLimit=0). Then there is a logarithm of zero during the computation. The problem can be signaled much earlier. The problem was signaled also by an user:

issue_title: Generate the hazard curves on the fly from the probability maps
issue_body: Currently the classical hazard calculator writes on the datastore both the probability maps and the hazard curves generated from them. The probability maps typically require few gigabytes of storage, while the hazard curves may require hundreds of gigabytes of storage. So, in order to scale, we need to remove the need to store the individual hazard curves and generate them on the fly only for a site at the time. It is fine to keep storing the statistical hazard curves, since they are few (typically we keep the mean and a couple of quantiles). This is needed for make the visualization on QGIS possible.

issue_title: Classifying transactions
issue_body:
Hello,

I get bank account statements in csv with a date and description of the transaction per row. 

I have a set of properly categorised transactions (previously imported transactions) and I would like to categorise new transactions based on the existing set of transactions. I wanted to do a process.extract(new_transaction.description, [List of existing_transaction.description]) and then assign to the new transaction the category of the best matched transaction.  
How can I speed up the process of categorising N transaction based on a set of M transactions ? I think at each run for a new transaction, fuzzywuzzy must reprocess the M transactions. Can we preprocessing/compile the M transactions to speed it ? Other ideas ?

issue_body:
Hello all,

We're currently facing a strange issue with pyvmomi over three different datacenter. VM creation is based on the [create_random_marvel_vms.py](https://github.com/vmware/pyvmomi-community-samples/blob/master/samples/create_random_marvel_vms.py) script. 

This is the code we've been using: 
```
task = destfolder.CreateVM_Task(config=configvm, pool=ds.config.entity)
tasks.wait_for_tasks(service_instance, [task])
```
On datacenter 1 and 2, everything is smooth and running fine. On the third datacenter (with exactly the same code), We face the following error:
```
Caught vmodl fault : Invalid datastore path '[DS2856_SHARED_SILVER_VNX_208] vm-name'.
```
The problem could be from the ESX? Maybe, but all shares the exact same API version:

**DC1: WORKS FINE**
Full name: VMware ESXi 5.1.0 build-1065491
Version: 5.1.0
API Version: 5.1

**DC2: WORKS FINE**
Full name: VMware ESXi 5.1.0 build-2323236
Version: 5.1.0
API Version: 5.1

**DC3: _DOESN'T WORK _**

API Version: 5.1

The Datastore on DC3 has the following Summary:
```
-------> (vim.Datastore.Summary) {

On the vcenter logs, we found the following:
```
2016-02-09T12:26:24.290+01:00 [25928 info 'commonvpxLro' opID=7cd6d19b] [VpxLRO] -- BEGIN task-internal-6407132 --  -- vmodl.query.PropertyCollector.retrievePropertiesEx -- c9462fd4-c987-8158-a2b9-47678ce6ab19(52719c15-a71d-dcc7-dccf-9cf4a7f7b2ae)

issue_title: Replace expressions with `setFeatureState` for choropleth and linestring vector tile visualizations
issue_body:
# Motivation

The data-join technique can be used in choropleth and linestring visualizations to join metadata to geometry in vector tiles over a unique identifier (or primary key).  The data-join technique currently uses expressions to do the join, which is slow to execute when joining more than 10k or so features.

# New feature

Use map.setFeatureState() released in GL JS v0.46 to perform the data-join technique.  map.setFeatureState() requires a unique integer `id` value to be included with the vector tile source.  Here's an example - https://bl.ocks.org/ryanbaumann/733ba99c5ca1d9d15259081b395e4b00

# Issues

* Since setFeatureState supports `id` values only, vector tiles must be created with this `id` in them in order to use setFeatureState().
* `id` values only support integers, so there would need to be an intermediate `mapping` table to join data between string values and integer `id` values.
* The difference between expressions and setFeatureState() data-join techniques should be hidden from the end user of the visualization library.

issue_title: Allowing multiple python versions
issue_body:
Hi,

I'd like to allow a project on multiple python versions. 

##### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

In the current case, my production environments are in 3.7 (docker image `python:slim-stretch`) while python 3.7 is not yet available in my dev environments (archlinux).
But I'm pretty sure that my project will work equally on 3.6 and 3.7, and would like to avoid installing and using another interpreter.
Also, I am using features like f-strings which are not available in 3.5, so I can't specify `python_version ="3"`, because on other dev environments like xenial, one must install an interpreter >=3.6.

##### Describe the solution you'd like

Allowing a version string in `Pipfile`, like:
```
[requires]
python_version = ">=3.6"
```

We could also allow a list, like `python_version = "3.6,3.7"`.

issue_title: pipenv does not install all extras
issue_body:
Pipenv seems to process only the first element in a list of extras.

Given a folder with the following Pipfile (otherwise empty):
```
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[dev-packages]

[packages]
apache-airflow = {extras = ["s3", "druid"], version = "==1.8.2"}

[requires]
python_version = "3.5"
```
`pipenv install` installs 53 packages. The package `pydruid`, which is defined in extra druid, is missing.

Given this Pipfile with inverted order of extras:
```
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[dev-packages]

[packages]
apache-airflow = {extras = ["druid", "s3"], version = "==1.8.2"}

[requires]
python_version = "3.5"
```

`pipenv install` installs 52 packages. The packages `boto` and `filechunkio`, which are defined in extra s3, are missing.



##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type: Mac OS 10.13.1
1. Python version: Python 2.7.10
1. Pipenv version: pipenv, version 8.3.2

issue_title: change_prefix should ignore case on case-insensitive file systems
issue_body:
On Windows, people can end up with a `PYTHONPATH` that leads to:

```Python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Python27\Scripts\virtualenv-script.py", line 9, in <module>
    load_entry_point('virtualenv==1.5.2', 'console_scripts', 'virtualenv')()
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 558, in main
    prompt=options.prompt)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 647, in create_environment
    site_packages=site_packages, clear=clear))
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 771, in install_python
    copy_required_modules(home_dir)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 725, in copy_required_modules
    dst_filename = change_prefix(filename, dst_prefix)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 710, in change_prefix
    (filename, prefixes)
AssertionError: Filename c:\Python27\Lib\os.py does not start with any of these prefixes: ['C:\\Python27']
```

where `PYTHONPATH` is set to `c:\Python27;c:\Python27\Lib` (lowercase `c`). The function should really use `os.path.normcase()` on the prefixes and the filename.

issue_title: Virtualenv seems to have overwritten system Python
issue_body:
Today I was trying to create a Python 2.7 virtualenv, but when I did, it replaced my Python 3.5.0 binaries with Python 2.7.10 binaries. Thereafter, I had no usable Python 3 on my system.

```
$ python3.5
Python 2.7.10 (default, Aug 22 2015, 20:33:39) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.0.0 (clang-700.0.59.1)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> ^D
$ which python3.5
/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/bin/python3.5
$ python3.5 -c "import sys; print(sys.prefix)"
/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7
```

As you can see, I'm using OS X. I had installed Python 3.5.0 using the OS X installer from python.org. I had `alias python='python3'` in my shell. I also had originally had virtualenv installed in my Python 3.5 environment, but it wasn't working so I installed it to my Python 2.7 environment as a user package.

When I invoked virtualenv, I used something like `virtualenv --python python2.7 ../.env` where `virtualenv` probably resolved to the broken script in the Python 3 environment.

Unfortunately, I wasn't paying that much attention when it happened, so I'm unsure all the details. I'm going to try to replicate it, but for now, I just wanted to register this highly unusual and unexpected behavior.

issue_title: [writer] Implement full-weight scalar style resolver.
issue_body:
It should resolve scalars to all available YAML scalar styles: [plain](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2788859), [single-quoted](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2788097), [double-quoted](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2787109), [folded](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2796251), and [literal](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2795688).

Examples:
``` yaml
text: Simple string.
```
``` yaml
text: 'String with trailing spaces...  '
```
``` yaml
text: "String with unprintable (\0, \uFFFE) characters."
```
``` yaml
text: "String with \"quotation\" 'mark' characters."
```
``` yaml
# Folded style: there is only one line break after the title.
text: >
  Multiline string.

  This is very a long
  text. It contains no
  line breaks. So it
  is better to use
  folded style here.
```
``` yaml
# Literal style: there is a line break after _each_ actual line.
text: |
  This text contains
  a lot of short lines.
  So, it is better to
  use literal style here.
```

Note. The actual resolver makes only rough choose between plain and double-quoted styles.

issue_title: Improve test suite for hessian_matrix_*
interpolation.pxd:  split to separate .pxd/.pyx ?
issue_body:
Is there a good reason that the shared cython interpolation code is stored within `_shared/interpolation.pxd ` itself rather than having just the declarations in the .pxd and the actual code in a separate interpolation.pyx file?  It seems like splitting into two files would avoid duplicating the C code each time a different .pyx file includes functions from interpolation.pxd.  

For example, denoise_cy.pyx uses only get_pixel3d from that file, but *all* functions in the pxd get included and compiled in denoise_cy.c.

issue_title: unwrap_phase raises ValueError when passed a masked array with no masked elements
issue_body:
The **unwrap_phase** function in the skimage.restoration namespace raises a ValueError when passed a masked array with a "shrunk" mask (containing only False).  For example:

```Python
import numpy as np
from skimage.restoration import unwrap_phase
image = np.ma.arange(100, dtype='float32').reshape(10, 10)
image[:] = np.ma.masked
unwrap_phase(image)
```

Extracting the data and mask from the image parameter should be done with the  **np.ma.getdata** and **np.ma.getmaskarray** functions and not by accessing the .mask and .data attributes directly.

issue_title: Update api docs for InferenceShifter
TM: Synapse Creation on every permanence adjustment
issue_body:
Not sure about what is going on with Python but I noticed something that could have tremendous memory pressure and performance impact if I'm right about what I'm seeing...?


In connections.py https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/research/connections.py#L194


``` Python
def updateSynapsePermanence(self, synapse, permanence):
    """
    Updates the permanence for a synapse.

    @param synapse    (int)   Synapse index
    @param permanence (float) New permanence
    """
    self._validatePermanence(permanence)

    data = self._synapses[synapse]
    newData = SynapseData(data.segment,   <--------------------   HERE
                          data.presynapticCell,
                          permanence)
    self._synapses[synapse] = newData

    # Update indexes
    self._synapsesForPresynapticCell[newData.presynapticCell][synapse] = newData
```

Every time the permanence is adjusted on a Synapse, a new SynapseData object is created?

(the line starting with: "newData = SynapseData(...)" )

When running a simple test in a short lived method such as extensive_temporal_memory_test.py I count something upwards of around 700 thousand Synapses! ...and 60 thousand Segments!

This line is called from "adaptSegment()" (line ~617) - EVERY TIME ADAPT SEGMENT IS CALLED!

I'm not sure how good the garbage collection is in Python (or even if it has garbage collection), but  this data should be indexed in a map and not created upon every adjustment in my opinion?

This needs to be a mutation instead of creation...?

issue_title: SparseMatrix methods copy their inputs
issue_body:
Every single `NumpyVectorT` in [sparse_matrix.i](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/5055a73c0a9eb8d3c52051b7f023c75605fabed4/src/nupic/bindings/sparse_matrix.i) is allocating a new vector. So whenever we put the inputs into a NumpyVectorT, we're copying the values, even when the values are already in a numpy array with the correct dtype.

It appears this comment is incorrect: https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/5055a73c0a9eb8d3c52051b7f023c75605fabed4/src/nupic/py_support/NumpyVector.hpp#L167

When I remove this unnecessary copying for the SparseMatrix methods that we use, I get a nice speed boost.

issue_title: Add goals etc. to README
issue_body:
I suggest we add a few goals and policies to the README. Mainly this:

- We aim to support what is in Bootstrap 4.
- Anything not natively part of Bootstrap 4 will not be accepted into `django-bootstrap4`, but plugins are welcome.

The reason for this is that I've seen a lot of cool PR's and suggestions for `django-bootstrap3` that enable `table` based views, date pickers, et cetera. It would be good that people know that the goal of the app is limited to Bootstrap 4.

Hi,

I was running tests on my code today involving Mailjet API calls and was surprised to get this warning:

issue_title: WebSearch: HTTP APIs to query available search fields/indexes, rankings, etc.
issue_body:
Originally on 2012-03-09

Along with the (soon-to-be) added HTTP APIs to retrieve the available collections of an Invenio-based repo, we could also add support for querying some additional information to launch a query via the search APIs. For eg, it should be possible to query about:

 - available fields/indexes (*author*, *title*, *language*, etc)
 - available ranking methods (*citation*, *wrd*, etc.)
 - available output formats (*xm*, *hb*, *hx*, etc.)
 - available languages (*en*, *it*, *de*, etc.)
 - etc. (?)

The returned structured (json?) should possibly contain a description for each value.

The above values could also be returned as part of the information sent along with the available collection list, as these values might also vary based on the collection. Alternatively, we could offer the possibility to filter the retrieved values per collection. For eg. the Invenio connector (client) could be augmented with the following functions:

```
def get_fields(c=None, ln=None):
   """
   Returns list of available search index, and their mapping to MARC fields:
   {'title': ['titolo', ['245__a', '246__b', '111__a']],
    'abstract': ['riassunto', ['520__%', '590__%']],
    ...
   }
   @param c: list of fields specifically relevant for given collection
   @param ln: language for the description. If not specified, default language 
              of the repository
   """
   [...]


def get_ranking_methods(c=None, ln=None):
   """
   Returns list of available ranking methods:
   {'wrd': 'word similarity',
    'citation': ''times cited',
    ...
   }
   @param c: list of rankings specifically relevant for given collection
   @param ln: language for the description. If not specified, default language 
              of the repository
   """
   [...]

def get_output_formats(c=None, ln=None):
   """
   Returns list of available output formats:
   {'hb': 'HTML brief',
    'hx': ''BibTeX',
    ...
   }
   @param c: list of output formats specifically relevant for given collection
   @param ln: language for the description. If not specified, default language 
              of the repository
   """
   [...]

def get_languages():
   """
   Returns list of available output languages
   {'en': 'English',
    'de': 'Deutsch',
    'it': 'Italiano',
    ...
   }
   """
   [...]
```

In the above examples one could imagine removing the *ln* parameter and returning all variants in the same call, to minimize number of requests.

It could also be made mandatory to authenticate to use these API, in order to invite developers to use these APIs with care (eg. with client-side caching).

issue_title: BibCirculation: problem with 'Edit physical copies'
issue_body:
When accessing the 'Item details' page of an item by e.g. pressing the 'Edit physical copies' button in bibedit a '500  Internal Server Error' is shown and an error mail is sent detailing an exception 
'HTTPError: HTTP Error 400: Bad Request' from urllib2 originating from the `BIBCIRCULATION_OPENER.open` call in the `get_book_cover(isbn)` function in bibcirculation_utils.py, lines 104ff. This call tries to retrieve a book cover for the item and is not essential for the functionality. (In Invenio 1.0 this problem did not arise because in the underlying code urllib instead if urllib2 had been used which throws no HTTPError exception.)

In the master branch this problem seems to be fixed by putting the BIBCIRCULATION_OPENER call into """ """, lines 179ff. A possible solution might be to back port this into the maint-1.1 branch.

I was able to get the BibCirculation working in our 1.1.4 test installation by porting the get_book_cover() function from master, i.e.
`"""cover_xml = BIBCIRCULATION_OPENER.open(...`
`...`
`...                 'Keywords=' + isbn)"""`
`cover_xml=""`
`...`
`except:`  (instead of `except AttributeError:` important!)

```
from datetime import datetime
from calendar import timegm
from rest_framework_jwt.settings import api_settings

def jwt_payload_handler(client_id):
    """ Custom payload handler
    Token encrypts the dictionary returned by this function, and can be decoded by rest_framework_jwt.utils.jwt_decode_handler

issue_title: Corrupted window size with Window.softinput_mode = 'below_target'
issue_body:
On Android platform, if Window.softinput_mode is set to 'below_target', the window size is corrupted (Window content is shifted to top of 20 or 30 lines depending on device model). 
This occurs **even if the keyboard is not used**. 
This issue **not** occurs with others softinput_mode.
This issue seems related with issue #544.
This code runs nice on Linux platform and was **not** tested on Windows, OS X or iOS.
The same buggy behavior is encountered with both Kivy 1.9.0 and Kivy 1.9.1.
This code was tested on 2 different devices with Android 6.0 Marshmallow (HTC m8 and Nexus 7).
The tool chain used was buildozer 0.31 with the following parameters:
```
android.api = 14
android.minapi = 8
android.sdk = 21
android.ndk = 10e
```
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8

from kivy.uix.boxlayout import BoxLayout
from kivy.uix.label import Label
from kivy.app import App
from kivy.core.window import Window

class MyClass(BoxLayout):

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(MyClass, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        softinput_mode = 'below_target'
        Window.softinput_mode = softinput_mode

class MyApp(App):
    def build(self):
        text = """
        Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit,
        sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.
        Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut
        aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit
        in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.
        Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident, sunt in culpa qui
        officia deserunt mollit anim id est laborum"""
        setattr(self, 'text', text)
        
        return MyClass()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    my_app = MyApp()
    my_app.run()
```

```Kv

<MyClass>:
    BoxLayout:
        id: main_layout

issue_title: Mechanism to record error without failing the test immediately
issue_body:
In system level tests, multiple errors could occur in a test.
Yet we want the test to proceed with the remaining steps when an error occurs, and eventually fail at the end.

For example, in a two device test, if one device's teardown failed, we want the other device to tear down properly; or if one teardown step failed, we want the remaining teardown steps to still happen. 
Another example, in a two device test, if a step is required for both devices, and this step is parallelized in different threads, error may happen in either or both threads.

For all these cases, we want to report all the errors that happened during the test in the end and mark the test as "Error".

In order to do this, Mobly could provide APIs for a test to record errors, fail the test if any error was recorded, and record all the errors as "Extra Errors"

issue_title: Negative array access on state->mtf for InverseMoveToFrontTransform
issue_body:
When decoding quickfox_repeated.compressed
The local variable `index` can start off zero, which will result in an access to s.mtf[-1]
https://github.com/google/brotli/blob/master/dec/decode.c#L881

Since the previous variable in the struct is a 32 bit integer mtf_upper_bound, and usually little endian < 2^24, this generally results in a zero access.

I believe this could exhibit itself as an issue on big endian systems and could be undefined behavior nevertheless. I think it would be more intuitive to restructure the code to avoid out of bounds access.

issue_title: Confusing behavior on typos when keyword args are reinterpreted as positional arguments
issue_body:
Let's say I have some function like this:

```
def myfunction(arg1, arg2, run_encoding):
     print(arg1, arg2, run_encoding)

fire.Fire()
```

If I accidentally misspell the run_encoding argument when I pass it as a positional parameter, I get behavior that can be really confusing to debug (e.g., if that's a relative file path, you suddenly get a `--mypath=1` directory):

```
›› python fire_example.py myfunction --arg1=a --arg2=b --run-encoding=5
('a', 'b', 5)
›› python fire_example.py myfunction --arg1=a --arg2=b --runencoding=5                                                                                                           
('a', 'b', '--runencoding=5')
```

I assume this happens because fire fills in all the keyword args and then assumes you specify positional arguments after them?

What I wish happened instead is that, unless explicitly quoted, any argument starting with `--` would be treated as a keyword arg, so you'd see:

```
1. Initial component
2. Accessed property "myfunction"
3. ('The function received no value for the required argument:', 'run_encoding')

Type:        function
String form: <function myfunction at 0x110138cf8>
File:        /Users/jtratner/fire_example.py
Line:        3

Usage:       fire_example.py myfunction ARG1 ARG2 RUN_ENCODING
             fire_example.py myfunction --arg1 ARG1 --arg2 ARG2 --run-encoding RUN_ENCODING
```

This is related to the issue in https://github.com/google/python-fire/issues/25, in that if positional arguments were not allowed after keyword arguments, you wouldn't end up with the ambiguity, but I can see how that's less "shell like".

issue_title: Nominatim: view_box parameter for Nominatim instance only works with string-represented numerical tuple
issue_body:
Hi and thanks for all your collective work on geopy. Really appreciate it and super helpful for data analytics! 

(Note, as an aside, that it's a little weird that the tuple is (longitude1, latitude1, longitude2, latitude2) instead of leading each 2-element pair within the 4-element tuple with latitude, but that seems to be [Nominatim's convention](https://github.com/openstreetmap/Nominatim/blob/81a7ea36db7d3b055acf25a36d2ea2c27af74765/test/bdd/api/search/params.feature), so it makes sense to keep it that way for the Class's parameters in geopy too - so that's not what this issue is about.)

Anyhow, the view_box parameter is a 4-element tuple of positive or negative integer or floating point data defining opposite corners of a rectangle in a 4-element python tuple(). If you use numerical data to create the tuple, you receive an error:

Setup and Call: 
```
box_tuple = tuple([180, 22, -180, -22])
geolocator = Nominatim(view_box=box_tuple)
location2 = geolocator.geocode("Peru")
```
 
Error:
```
~/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/geopy/geocoders/osm.py in geocode(self, query, exactly_one, timeout, addressdetails, language, geometry)
    161         # `viewbox` apparently replaces `view_box`
    162         if self.view_box:
--> 163             params['viewbox'] = ','.join(self.view_box)
    164 
    165         if self.country_bias:

TypeError: sequence item 0: expected str instance, int found
```

If you use string data to create the tuple, no error, and the call to geocode() works as expected, resulting in a location returned that lies within the defined boundaries.

Setup and Call: 
```
box_tuple = tuple(["180", "22", "-180", "-22"])
geolocator = Nominatim(view_box=box_tuple)
location2 = geolocator.geocode("Peru")
location2
```

Output:
`Location(Perú, (-6.8699697, -75.0458515, 0.0))`

FWIW, my suggested fix would be to convert a numerical tuple to strings somehow, but I am not sure what the most efficient implementation or input handling would be, which is why I'm submitting this as an issue instead of coding an implementation.

I'm using geopy 1.11.0 with a Python 3.6.2 in a jupyter notebook.

Please let me know if I've been unclear or you need more info or have other questions. Thanks again, so much for all your work on this.

issue_title: raven.contrib.celery.register_logger_signal ignores subclasses of SentryHandler
issue_body:
`raven.contrib.celery.register_logger_signal` attempts to find an existing handler to add a CeleryFilter to.  It only considers existing handlers that are of type: `raven.handlers.logging.SentryHandler`.  This means that if a subclass of this handler is found (such as `raven.contrib.django.handlers.SentryHandler`) the existing handler is ignored and a brand new handler with default settings is added, resulting in two potentially duplicate handlers.

The fix for this would be to change the `type` check [here](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/contrib/celery/__init__.py#L46) to an `isinstance` check.

I'm interested in submitting a PR for this with tests, if this change is considered appropriate.

Here is the skeleton of a test that would help cover this:
```
def test_subclassed_handler_updated(self):
    client = InMemoryClient()
    register_logger_signal(client)

    class CustomHandler(SentryHandler):
        pass

    root = logging.getLogger()
    handler = CustomHandler(client)
    root.addHandler(handler)

    celery.signals.after_setup_logger.send(
        sender=None, logger=root,
        loglevel=logging.WARNING, logfile=None,
        format=u'', colorize=False,
    )

    assert len(root.handlers) == 1

    handler = root.handlers[0]
    assert isinstance(handler, CustomHandler)

    assert len(handler.filters) == 1

    _filter = handler.filters[0]
    assert isinstance(_filter, CeleryFilter)
```

issue_title: Raven should not call functions on half-initialized objects.
issue_body:
If an exception that was raised in a constructor is reported to sentry, raven will try to serialize all variables in the stacktrace, including the `self` in the constructor.

This results in raven calling `__repr__()` on uninitialized objects, which is definitely not safe. Raven should not try to serialize half-initialized objects (=object that are `self` in a function `__init__` somewhere in the stacktrace), but instead report something like `Unitialized[Classname]`.

The basic issue with this can be seen like this:
```
try:
    uuid.UUID('invalid')
except Exception:
    sentry_client.captureException()
```

https://github.com/ansible/awx/issues/1858

##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Bug Report

##### COMPONENT NAME
 - API

##### SUMMARY
Getting a list of jobs that are running and have an elapsed time greater than N seconds does not seem to work.

##### ENVIRONMENT
* AWX version: Tower 3.2.2

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

Pasting tower-cli commands used for easiness, I get the same bevaviour if I point a browswer of my choice at `https://tower.example.com/api/v2/jobs/?&elapsed__gt=9;status=running` and `https://tower.example.com/api/v2/jobs/?&elapsed__gt=9&status=running` (unsure if *;* or *&* needed when combining the query. I'm told *;* is the correct delimiter)

(It's just easier to paste one shell session than copypasta from multiple queries in the browser)

[vagrant@tower ~]$ tower-cli job list --all-pages --query status running

issue_title: Proposal for more fine-grained query contract type validations
issue_body:
This is simply an informal nice-to-have proposal to evaluate doing more fine-grained type assertions as part of the validation layer based on API input contract annotations via OpenAPI specification. 

This might partially break the Postel's Law about robustness principle, but it is certainly convenient for some kind of APIs where consumer are mostly humans and error-prone while consuming the API.

Some of the scenarios it would be nice having covered would be:

- for integer types if we pass each of the following values `["foo", None, True, False, [], {}, (), 234.243527, 0.0]`, the values `True` and `234.243527` are valid, but the rest all return the error message `limit must be an integer`. 0.0 returns no results, but other floats do not.

- for a list type of string items, if we pass each of the following values `[None, True, False, {}, 9, 234.24, 0.0, -1, []]`, the values `[None]`, `[[]]`, and `[{}]` works instead of no results (or a validation `400` message)

Those inconsistencies were discovered by our QA team while testing the APIs. I'm sure there could be more if we do more fuzzing testing.

What do you think this proposal?
I'm open to collaborate if the proposal has serious chances to be included within the project.

issue_title: Migrer les barèmes IPP des fichiers Excel vers le format YAML des paramètres openfisca
issue_body:
Contexte:

L'IPP est convaincu qu'une source unique de vérité des paramètres de législation serait très utile en général et notamment pour le projet openfisca.
L'IPP est également convaincu que le format Excel n'est pas adapté au contrôle de version ni à l'édition collaborative et à un suivi des validations. 

Cependant les barèmes IPP actuels présentaient les avantages suivants:
  - Les paramètres sont rassemblés par types qui font sens pour les consommateurs
  - Ils sont sourcés (référence législatives) et accompagnées de notes utiles   
  - Une majorité des consommateurs des barèmes IPP apprécient le format Excel
  - Ce dernier point n'est pas étranger aux nombreux retours et suggestions d'améliorations (qui arrivent parfois sous forme de fichiers excel amendés)

Proposition:

 - Migrer les Barèmes IPP Excel vers des fichiers YAML de parmaètres openfisca.
 - Améliorer le format pour qu'il puisse contenir toute l'information fournie par les Barèmes IPP.
 - Fournir un moyen de consommer les paramètres sous forme de fichiers csv et/ou Excel.
 - Explorer l'élaboration d'une interface d'édition/signalement d'erreur des paramètres pour les consommateurs avancés.
 - L'IPP produira sa "vue" des Barèmes IPP sur sont site pour reproduire les divisions thématiques présentes dans les fichiers Excel afin de préserver cette valeur ajoutée et ne pas dérouter ses consommateurs actuels.

Proposition de Roadmap
 - Améliorer le format YAML openfisca-parameters pour inclure les champ références législatives, Parution au JO, notes etc (communauté + IPP)
 - parser entièrement _tous_ les barèmes IPP (communauté + IPP)
 - explorer the améliorations des fonctionnalités des paramètres IPP afin de permettre le téléchargement de séries sous divers formats etc (communauté + IPP) 
 - élaboration de la vue IPP pour remplacer définitivement les fichiers Excel (IPP)
  
Questions à régler:
  - Généralement un feuillet rassemble plusieurs paramètres qui sont organisés en colonnes. Chaque ligne est une date de changement d'au moins un paramètres et contient une ou plusieurs références législatives.  L'extraction des dates de changement par paramètres et de la bonne référence législatives n'est donc pas évident.

Autres références:
 - #852
 - #837

Commentaires bienvenus ! @maukoquiroga @Anna-Livia @sandcha @guillett @fpagnoux

issue_title: Update libbson to 1.6
issue_body:
[Libbson 1.6](https://github.com/mongodb/libbson/releases/tag/1.6.0) included some major improvements to it's bson to json encoding:
- Use jsonsl instead of libyajl as our JSON parsing library, parse JSON more
strictly, fix minor parsing bugs.
- Extended JSON documents like '{"$code": "...", "$scope": {}}' are now parsed
into BSON "code" elements.
- ISO8601 dates now allow years from 0000 to 9999 inclusive. Before, years
before 1970 were prohibited.
- BSON floats and ints are now distinguished in JSON output.

issue_title: Explicit broadcasting operation
issue_body:
Currently DyNet does not have an explicit broadcasting operation to duplicate tensors along a particular dimension. The interface would be something like `broadcast(Tensor t, unsigned dimension, unsigned multiplier)` or `broadcast(Tensor t, vector<unsigned> dimensions, vector<unsigned> multipliers)`.

Note that explicit broadcasting would result in a memory allocation and copy, and is not efficient, so implicit broadcasting operations like the ones stated here are preferable: https://github.com/clab/dynet/issues/440

However, there will always be some edge cases where the thing a user wants to do will not be implemented, so we should also give the ability to do so.

issue_title: devices[0] is not the CPU
restructuring weight decay?
issue_body:
I find the way we currently handle weight-decay / regularization to be:

1. very badly documented
2. confusing to work with

In particular, there are currently two places to introduce weight-decay:
- Through a commandline option
- Through `set_weight_decay_lambda` in a `ParameterCollection`

The second option does not seem to work as intended (see #1243 ),
and the first and second options go through separate code paths.
Additionally, one cannot assign different weight decay values to individual parameters.


I suggest to move to the following behavior:

- Remove the commandline weight-decay option.
- `ParameterCollection` gets a weight-decay-lambda value on time of creation.
   - for top-level `ParameterCollection`, the default is 0, and can be specified in the constructor.
   - for sub-collections, the default is inherited from parent, but can be overriden in the constructor.
- Each `Parameters`/`LookupParameters` also gets its own weight-decay-lambda value.
   - inherited from its parent.
   - and can be set manually through a `.set_weight_decay_lambda` method.
- The value the optimizers actually look at is on the `Parameters`/`LookupParameters`.

The above proposal removes `set_weight_decay_lambda` from `ParameterCollection`, but I am also OK with keeping it for backward-compatibility.

If we want to keep the commandline option, I suggest it will be implemented as affecting the default value of the top-level `ParameterCollection`, only.

Thoughts?

I can give a shot at implementing it, if there is agreement.

issue_title: pixel_to_skycoord and skycoord_to_pixel
issue_body:
Astropy has two nice convenience function for converting between pixels and `SkyCoord` objects (i.e. with a coordinate frame):
* `pixel_to_skycoord` (http://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.wcs.utils.pixel_to_skycoord.html)
* `skycoord_to_pixel` (http://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.wcs.utils.skycoord_to_pixel.html#astropy.wcs.utils.skycoord_to_pixel)

It would be nice if `gwcs` had similar functions.  Ideally, the existing astropy functions would be able to work with `gwcs` objects, so perhaps this issue falls under the "unified wcs" initiative.

issue_title: Emit header for gRPC messages
issue_body:
gRPC connections currently don't have enough information in the request for us to do efficient traffic management/routing. 

Will you add a header that allows for us to optimize for this on the server? The header format is:

```
name: google-cloud-resource-prefix
value: projects/my-project-id
```

In general, the prefix would be the same as the JSON-over-http URL up to any custom method. For example, a Cloud Datastore commit message over JSON would hit the url `projects/my-project-id:commit`.

Here is an example usage in the cloud-bigtable-client: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/cloud-bigtable-client/pull/915/files#diff-1078787e69bc1d7c5bb8507f0a87a143R83

issue_title: Allow easy paging for list operations
issue_body:
Taking the comment on https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/pull/889/files#r31042158 over here.

Requiring explicit paging for topics is really ugly, there must be a better way than:

``` python
all_topics = []

topics, token = client.list_topics()
all_topics.extend(topics)
while token is not None:
  topics, token = client.list_topics(page_token=token)
  all_topics.extend(topics)
```

If our concern is "people could make a boatload of requests and they don't realize it", we could always limit this like we do with the recursive deleting in storage?

``` python
topics = client.list_topics(limit=100)
```

We can do a bunch of this with the `page_size` parameter, but that might mean that I have to wait for _everything_ to come back before starting any work, which seems kind of ridiculous.

It'd be really nice if that limit and page-size stuff was both there, so it's easy to do things like "I want the first 5000 topics, and I want to pull them from the server in chunks of 50":

``` python
for topic in client.list_topics(page_size=50, limit=5000):
  push_work_to_other_system(topic)
```

---

To add a bit more context, I'd like to toss out: what if we made all of our list operations return iterators?

The use cases I see here are...
1. I want everything, give it all to me (`for topic in list_topics()`)
2. I want up to N things, stop giving them to me then (`for topic in list_topics(limit=100)`)
3. I don't know how many I want, I'll know when I want to stop though... (`for topics in list_topics(): if topic.name == 'foo': break`)
4. Combination of the previous two (I don't know when I want to stop, but don't let me go on forever, kill it at say... 1000)
5. I want to pick up where I left off, I saved a token somewhere (sort of like `offset`)! (`for topic in list_topics(page_token=token, limit=100)`)

The "let's just always return `page, page_token`" thing doesn't really make all of those use-cases all that fun... But if we always return iterators, they are all easy.

Further, let's say I have a weird case where I just want one page worth of stuff... `list_topics().get_current_page()` could return what you want, no?

issue_title: RemoteRpc leaks HTTP connections when handling Datastore errors
issue_body:
The exception handling code in `com.google.datastore.v1.client.RemoteRpc` does not close the HTTP content input stream after parsing the protobuf message `com.google.rpc.Status` (which describes the Datastore error).

Closing the content input stream allows the underlying `HttpTransport` to clean up resources (i.e. release the leased HTTP connection back to the connection pool). Without cleanup, alternative `HttpTransport` implementations (e.g. `ApacheHttpTransport`) experience connection timeouts as soon as they have depleted their connection pool.

issue_title: Parameter instantiated with template= adds itself as a resource
issue_body:
When you create a param by passing it a template object, such as
`MyParam = troposphere.Parameter("MyParam", template=t, ...)`
it is added to the template as a resource, not a parameter, as happens when instantiated as follows
`MyParam = template.add_parameter(troposphere.Parameter("MyParam", ...))`

`troposphere.Parameter` extends `troposphere.BaseAWSObject` (through `AWSDeclaration`).
`BaseAWSObject.__init__` does the following
```
if self.template is not None:
    self.template.add_resource(self)
```
This means Parameters and Outputs (not sure if there are other types?) add themselves as resources.

Would the best solution to be to break the above code snippet into its own `add_self_to_template` function so it can be overridden by Parameter and Output?

issue_title: SizeConstraint for WAF generating JSON with the parameters wrapped in Properties
issue_body:
While configuring rules for our CloudFront WAF, I encountered an error from CloudFormation due to the JSON generated for the SizeConstraint object.

The SizeConstraint object gets written as:

    {
        "Properties": {
            "ComparisonOperator": "GE",
            "FieldToMatch": {
                "Type": "BODY"
            },
            "Size": 256000,
            "TextTransformation": "NONE"
        }
    }

Based on CloudFormations documentation and the error output from CloudFormation those parameters should not be wrapped in a Properties object.

See the "Define a Size Constraint" section - 
http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-waf-sizeconstraintset.html 

Error from CloudFormation:

    Status: CREATE_FAILED, Type: AWS::WAF::SizeConstraintSet, Reason: Encountered unsupported property Properties

Here is what I had defined in troposphere:

    size_constraint_set = template.add_resource(SizeConstraintSet(
        "WafSizeConstraintSet",
        Name="WafSizeConstraintSet",
        SizeConstraints=[
            SizeConstraint(
                "WafSizeConstraint",
                ComparisonOperator="GE",
                FieldToMatch=FieldToMatch(
                    Type="BODY"
                ),
                Size=256000,
                TextTransformation="NONE"
            )
        ]
    ))

I've tested removing the "Properties" object, and CloudFormation accepted that.

issue_title: Design @rules that expose (scoped) Subsystems
issue_body:
Supporting Subsystem dependencies in `@rule`s will require porting some of options parsing into `@rule`s. The desired end-user API is straightforward... something like:
```
subsystem = yield Get(SomeSubsystem, Scope('some.scope'))
```

----

The challenging bit, though, will be how to allow for interaction with the environment variables and CLI args within a particular request to the engine without invalidating more of the graph than is necessary.

A very naive and non-concurrency-safe approach would be to install a `SingletonRule` for env and cli args that would be invalidated (along with its dependents) for every request if they had changed. In the absence of concurrency (and presence of #4558) this approach would work fine, because in cases where `@rule`s that parsed the env/CLI were not affected by the changes, the invalidation would be quite cheap. But in the presence of concurrency, it falls down entirely, because you cannot have any single node to represent arguments.

Instead, it seems like env and args might either be `Variants`, or some other form of "caller provided parameters"... cleaning up Variants to the point where they could support all of 1) codegen, 2) cross builds, 3) env/args... would be very exciting.

[jvm.repl.scala]
options: ["-Dscala.usejavacp=true" ]
```

Without it:
```console
$ ./pants repl src/java::
...
13:38:32 00:01     [scala]
Welcome to Scala version 2.10.4 (OpenJDK 64-Bit Server VM, Java 1.7.0_85).
Type in expressions to have them evaluated.
Type :help for more information.

scala> 
Failed to initialize compiler: object scala.runtime in compiler mirror not found.
** Note that as of 2.8 scala does not assume use of the java classpath.
** For the old behavior pass -usejavacp to scala, or if using a Settings
** object programatically, settings.usejavacp.value = true.
```

As the repl itself reports, all but ancient scala repls need the usejavacp option; so pants should default this option.

issue_title: Investigate a non-spot strategy for flexible Jenkins worker capacity
issue_body:
Internally at Foursquare, we have a fleet of on-demand workers created but left in the `STOP` state (so they are free minus the EBS volumes attached to them).  A python script run on the Jenkins master node uses a combination of the Jenkins REST API and boto3 to determine if there is queueing, and if so, move nodes from `STOP` to `START` state (and similarly stop nodes when there is no demand).

This is probably a bit more expensive than using spot pricing, but it is significantly more reliable and still quite cost efficient.  Also, the workers come up very fast if they are EBS-only SSDs nodes (instead of our strategy which uses the ephemeral SSD on the machine, which is lost on stop/start and must be reformatted and have repos recloned).

The current issues with the Jenkins spot plugin and market price fluctuation are a deal-breaker for functionality, and unless significantly better heuristics for selecting and bidding on instances based on price and capacity become available, we should probably switch to an on-demand setup.

Similarly the endTagHandler in the inBody phase incorrectly lists "dl" as one of the endTagListItem triggers; it's already being consumed by endTagBlock.

Since in both cases it's the first method that's correctly picked up, so doesn't actually have any result, but should be removed anyway. 

Thanks.

issue_title: Python 2.7 Support
issue_body:
I'd suggest adding python 2.7 support to enable use of this code by the wider humanitarian community.

It would be great to have `1 click` publishing of datasets to HDX from GeoNode systems (http://geonode.org/), which contain many datasets potentially useful on HDX.  For instance, a popup that appears asking if you would like to publish to HDX.

GeoNode is currently written in Python 2.7 (https://github.com/geonode/geonode).  GeoNode (as well as other systems) can only be upgraded to Python 3.0 once all their dependencies are upgraded, which could still be years away.

A standard python API that shields the complexity of CKAN's REST API is great!  However, to seamlessly integrate HDX publishing into workflows, the code will need to run directly within business systems rather than as a separate script.

issue_title: Restarted processes (via SIGUSR2) don't know about BGP session state
issue_body:
We use `SIGUSR2` to reload configuration and processes. One issue we are having is that it is important  for the child processes to know the BGP session state of the neighbour. For example, when we restart ExaBGP we get the `"state": "connected"` message. So the child processes are aware that the session is up. We also get `"state": "down"` messages to know when it is down. But when using `SIGUSR2` the processes come up with no idea about the state of the BGP session. It is basically unknown until some state change happens on the BGP session. This is because ExaBGP doesn't tell the restarted processes the current state.

Is the above intentional or have you just never had a need to make sure the child process has the current BGP session state? Any ideas on what we could do to determine the state within the restarted child processes?

issue_body:
<!---
Hello,

We are sorry that you are experiencing an issue with ExaBGP.

Before opening this issue could you please:
